learningOutcomes: Explain the accounting equation and double-entry bookkeeping
Record transactions in a journal with proper narration
Post journal entries to ledger accounts
realLifeContexts: Why every purchase must be recorded in at least two accounts
How a shopkeeper tracks what they owe and what is owed to them
Why accountants must balance debits and credits
commonMisconceptions: Debit always means money going out
Credit is always good and debit is always bad
The journal and ledger contain the same information in the same format

learningOutcomes: Classify accounts: personal, real, and nominal
Apply golden rules of accounting to complex transactions
Prepare a trial balance and identify errors
Distinguish between errors of omission, commission, and principle
realLifeContexts: Why a trial balance matching doesn't guarantee no errors
How a suspense account is used to locate accounting errors
Why classifying salaries as assets (error of principle) distorts financial statements
commonMisconceptions: A balanced trial balance means no errors exist
All errors affect both sides of the trial balance
Suspense account permanently remains in books if not resolved

learningOutcomes: Prepare journal entries for complex transactions including adjustments
Rectify errors using journal entries and suspense accounts
Apply accounting standards to recognition and measurement
Analyze the impact of accrual vs. cash basis on financial statements
realLifeContexts: Why a firm paying advance rent must not record it as current expense
How accrual accounting gives a truer picture than cash-based records
Why auditors require consistent accounting policies across years
commonMisconceptions: Accrual basis always overstates profit
Adjusting entries only apply to expenses
Once posted to ledger, entries cannot be corrected

learningOutcomes: Explain the purpose of a Trading and Profit & Loss Account
Describe what a Balance Sheet shows
Identify revenue and capital items
realLifeContexts: How a small business owner knows if they made profit or loss
Why a balance sheet is called a 'snapshot' of the business
How banks use balance sheets to decide on loans
commonMisconceptions: Profit equals cash in hand
Balance sheet includes only tangible items
Revenue expenses are always smaller than capital expenses

learningOutcomes: Prepare final accounts with adjustments (outstanding, prepaid, depreciation, provision)
Distinguish between capital and revenue receipts and expenditure
Explain the concept of gross profit and net profit margin
Prepare a Receipts and Payments Account for non-profit organizations
realLifeContexts: Why school fees received in advance appear as liabilities
How clubs prepare income and expenditure accounts instead of P&L
Why provision for bad debts reduces profits conservatively
commonMisconceptions: Receipts and Payments Account is the same as Income and Expenditure Account
Capital receipts increase profit
All outstanding items reduce profit

learningOutcomes: Prepare and analyze financial statements under Companies Act format
Apply Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS) to recognition and disclosure
Evaluate the quality of earnings and creative accounting practices
Interpret notes to accounts and contingent liabilities
realLifeContexts: How Satyam Computer Services manipulated financial statements
Why auditors check notes to accounts for hidden liabilities
How Ind AS convergence improved comparability with global firms
commonMisconceptions: Companies Act format is identical to Ind AS format
Contingent liabilities always appear on the face of the balance sheet
True and fair view means exact precision in all financial figures

learningOutcomes: Define depreciation and explain why it is charged
Distinguish between straight-line method and written-down value method
Calculate depreciation using both methods
realLifeContexts: Why a car bought for ₹8 lakh is worth less every year
Why computers need to be replaced even if physically working
How depreciation reduces taxable profit
commonMisconceptions: Depreciation represents actual cash paid out each year
Land is depreciated like other fixed assets
WDV method always results in lower depreciation than SLM

learningOutcomes: Prepare journal entries for depreciation and disposal of fixed assets
Distinguish between provisions, reserves, and accumulated depreciation
Explain depreciation fund method and insurance policy method
Account for revaluation and impairment of assets
realLifeContexts: How a bus company accounts for selling old buses
Why banks maintain provisions against non-performing assets (similar concept)
How asset revaluation affects balance sheet values
commonMisconceptions: Accumulated depreciation account is a liability
Profit on sale of fixed asset is operating income
Impairment and depreciation serve the same accounting purpose

learningOutcomes: Apply component depreciation under Ind AS 16
Assess the impact of depreciation policy on profit and tax
Evaluate IFRS vs. Indian GAAP treatment of assets
Analyze deferred tax arising from temporary differences in depreciation
keyTerms: tax depreciation (WDV under Income Tax Act)
realLifeContexts: Why a factory with multiple components depreciates each part separately
How deferred tax arises when accounting and tax depreciation rates differ
Why Ind AS allows fair value model for investment properties
commonMisconceptions: Ind AS 16 only applies to manufacturing companies
Component depreciation always reduces current year profit
Deferred tax liability means tax is never paid

learningOutcomes: Define partnership and explain features of a partnership deed
Prepare a capital account (fixed and fluctuating)
Distribute profit among partners according to profit-sharing ratio
realLifeContexts: How two friends starting a business together should document their agreement
Why partners who contribute more capital may receive interest as compensation
How a working partner gets salary even if profit ratio is equal
commonMisconceptions: Partners always share profit equally
Interest on capital is an expense debited to the firm's account directly
Partnership and company are the same type of business

learningOutcomes: Prepare Profit & Loss Appropriation Account
Account for admission of a new partner (goodwill, revaluation, capital adjustment)
Account for retirement and death of a partner
Calculate gaining and sacrificing ratio
realLifeContexts: How an existing business values its reputation when a new partner joins
Why a retiring partner must be paid accumulated reserves
How goodwill is adjusted when profit-sharing ratios change
commonMisconceptions: Goodwill is always cash paid when a new partner joins
Revaluation profit is distributed in old ratio always
A new partner always gets a share in all existing goodwill

learningOutcomes: Prepare accounts for dissolution of partnership firm
Apply Garner vs. Murray rule to capital deficiency
Account for piecemeal realization and maximum loss method
Evaluate conversion of partnership to a company
realLifeContexts: How assets are sold and liabilities settled when a firm closes
Why Garner vs. Murray affects solvent partners when one partner can't pay
How a growing partnership firm becomes a private limited company
commonMisconceptions: Realization account profit equals net profit of the year of dissolution
Garner vs. Murray applies to all partnerships regardless of agreement
Dissolution and retirement of all partners are the same

learningOutcomes: Distinguish between a company and a partnership
Explain share capital: equity and preference shares
Describe the process of issue of shares
realLifeContexts: Why Infosys shareholders receive dividends
How companies like Zomato raise money through IPO
Why preference shareholders get paid before equity shareholders
commonMisconceptions: Shares are the same as loans to a company
Preference shareholders always receive a guaranteed fixed dividend
Face value and market value of shares are always the same

learningOutcomes: Account for issue of shares at par, premium, and discount
Prepare journal entries for forfeiture and reissue of shares
Account for debenture issue and redemption
Prepare a company's final accounts under Schedule III
realLifeContexts: Why companies must maintain DRR before redeeming debentures
How forfeited shares are reissued at a discount to attract new investors
Why SEBI restricts issue of shares at discount
commonMisconceptions: Securities premium is distributable as dividend
Forfeited shares are permanently cancelled
All debentures must be redeemed by buying back from market

learningOutcomes: Account for buyback of shares and its regulatory requirements
Prepare consolidated financial statements (basic introduction)
Analyze ESOP accounting under Ind AS
Evaluate merger and acquisition accounting
realLifeContexts: Why Infosys and TCS regularly buy back their own shares
How Tata Sons consolidates subsidiaries in group accounts
ESOP schemes used by startups to attract and retain talent
commonMisconceptions: Buyback always increases earnings per share
Consolidated accounts simply add up all group company accounts
Goodwill on consolidation is the same as goodwill in partnership

learningOutcomes: Define ratio analysis and explain its purpose
Calculate basic liquidity ratios (current ratio, quick ratio)
Interpret what a ratio value indicates about financial health
realLifeContexts: Why a bank checks current ratio before approving a business loan
How investors compare two companies using ratios
Why a current ratio below 1 signals potential cash problems
commonMisconceptions: A higher ratio is always better
Current ratio and quick ratio give the same information
Ratio analysis tells us everything about a company's future

learningOutcomes: Calculate and interpret profitability, solvency, and activity ratios
Use DuPont analysis to decompose return on equity
Compare ratios across firms and over time
Identify limitations of ratio analysis
realLifeContexts: Why Reliance and Tata Steel have different appropriate debt-equity ratios
How DuPont analysis reveals whether ROE improvement is sustainable
Why inventory turnover matters more for FMCG than for capital goods firms
commonMisconceptions: A low debt-equity ratio is always safe and conservative
Ratios are objective measures not subject to accounting policy choices
Industry comparison of ratios is unnecessary if year-on-year trend is good

learningOutcomes: Apply Altman Z-score for bankruptcy prediction
Analyze quality of earnings through accruals ratio
Evaluate off-balance-sheet items and their ratio distortions
Assess ESG metrics integration with financial ratio analysis
realLifeContexts: How Altman Z-score predicted airline bankruptcies before COVID
Why Amazon's low net profit margin doesn't mean poor performance (FCF analysis)
How operating lease adjustments affect airline leverage ratios
commonMisconceptions: EBITDA is a reliable cash flow proxy
Altman Z-score is universally applicable across all industries
Off-balance-sheet items are always disclosed in notes to accounts

learningOutcomes: Explain why cash flow statement is prepared alongside P&L
Distinguish between operating, investing, and financing activities
Define cash and cash equivalents
realLifeContexts: Why a profitable company can still run out of cash
How a startup burns cash even while growing rapidly
Why investors look at free cash flow rather than just profit
commonMisconceptions: A profitable company never faces cash shortage
Cash flow statement and P&L statement show the same information
All operating activities involve cash immediately

learningOutcomes: Prepare cash flow statement using direct and indirect method
Adjust net profit for non-cash items and working capital changes
Classify complex transactions correctly into activities
Interpret cash flow patterns to assess business health
realLifeContexts: How Amazon's high capex shows in investing activities despite high operating cash flow
Why depreciation is added back in indirect method
How cash flow patterns differ between a startup and a mature firm
commonMisconceptions: Indirect method adjusts P&L profit to get cash, not actual cash receipts
Depreciation is a cash outflow because it reduces profit
Positive cash from financing always means the company is growing

learningOutcomes: Reconstruct missing figures using cash flow statement analysis
Apply free cash flow to firm (FCFF) and to equity (FCFE) in valuation
Evaluate cash flow manipulation and earnings management signs
Assess capital allocation quality using cash flow metrics
realLifeContexts: How Enron manipulated operating cash flows by securitizing receivables
Warren Buffett's focus on owner earnings (FCF) over reported profit
Cash conversion cycle in retail vs. capital goods sectors
commonMisconceptions: Free cash flow equals net profit minus capex always
Companies cannot manipulate cash flow statements
High operating cash flow always indicates a high-quality business

learningOutcomes: Explain why inventory valuation matters
Describe FIFO and LIFO methods
Calculate closing stock under each method
realLifeContexts: How a grocery shop prices older stock vs. newer stock
Why inventory value affects reported profit
How rising prices affect COGS differently under FIFO vs. LIFO
commonMisconceptions: FIFO means first items produced are always sold first in practice
LIFO is allowed under Indian GAAP and IFRS equally
Inventory method choice doesn't affect profit significantly

learningOutcomes: Apply FIFO, LIFO, and weighted average to perpetual and periodic systems
Explain the lower of cost or net realizable value (NRV) rule
Account for damaged, obsolete, and slow-moving inventory
Calculate and interpret inventory turnover ratio
realLifeContexts: Why fashion retailers must write down unsold seasonal stock
How supermarkets use perpetual inventory via barcode scanning
How inventory write-downs affected Yes Bank's NPA provisions (analogy)
commonMisconceptions: NRV is always less than cost
Periodic inventory is less accurate because it uses physical count only
Inventory write-downs only occur in manufacturing

learningOutcomes: Apply Ind AS 2 requirements for inventory recognition and measurement
Analyze the impact of inventory method changes on comparability
Evaluate inventory financing and supply chain accounting
Assess standard costing vs. actual costing for inventory management
realLifeContexts: Why LIFO is prohibited under Ind AS 2 and IFRS
How agricultural companies value inventory under biological asset rules
Why auto manufacturers use standard costing for massive production inventories
commonMisconceptions: Ind AS 2 allows management to choose any cost formula freely
Standard costing always approximates actual cost for Ind AS 2 purposes
Abnormal wastage is included in inventory cost under Ind AS 2
